title: A note on Simon Boccanegra
body:
The prelude had me hooked immediately. I love music from the Romantic period. Those first gentle and restrained string harmonies, before the curtain was even lifted, drew me right in. It made the theatre feel small, in the best way, like the room had shrunk around the orchestra.

I had a great seat too, a view that showed the conductor working both the orchestra and singers on stage at the same time, and it was genuinely impressive to see. I also got to witness a very wholesome moment when the cymbal guy came on to do his short part, then clapped his mates on the shoulder whilst walking past them to leave the orchestra pit.

I’d read the synopsis beforehand, so I knew roughly what to expect, and in that sense, the opera unfolded the way I thought an opera “should.” The staging itself, though, I found a little boring; it did the job, but not particularly memorable. I did very much enjoy the lighting changing through the skylights to indicate the time of day, though, that was cool.

What did stay with me were the chorus moments. I hadn’t really experienced that before at an opera: the sound of tens of singers resonating through the whole theatre, especially when they sang offstage. Combined with the tolling of the bells during Boccanegra’s death scene, it made it feel very immersive. The supporting cast was great throughout, actually. The funeral-goers, the little girls, the countless plebeians and patricians all did a great job bringing it to life.

Strangely enough, the Council Chamber Act is where my attention started to drift. Everything before it had felt interconnected, flowing from one scene to the next, but then this was plonked in. A totally different style from everything we had seen so far. Interesting because afterwards I learned that the scene was added later, in an 1881 revision to the 1857 original. Verdi had had 24 years to hone his craft, and you could tell. It was extremely dense and technically challenging. Perhaps it’s just my baby opera brain feeling a bit overwhelmed that made it feel out of place.

Simon, as a character, I felt sorry for. He’s thrust into a role above his station, and then resented for it later by the very people around him. There’s something sad about being punished for a status you didn’t fully choose. Also, having recently watched Le Nozze di Figaro, it seems like rediscovered or confused parentage is a recurring theme in Italian opera. Simon and Amelia fit right in.

There’s something almost utopian about Simon as a leader, though: a man who tries to reconcile with his enemies, who searches for his lost child instead of just consolidating power. Real leaders today don’t really look like that. Maybe that’s part of why the story still got to me.

All in all, a wonderful introduction to an opera I knew almost nothing about a week ago. It was the final performance of the run, and you could hear the cast celebrating and clapping on stage behind the curtain as we all filtered out. A nice reminder that behind the conflict, grief and politics we’d just watched, there was a group of people celebrating the creation of something spectacular, together.

Seen at Nationale Opera & Ballet, Amsterdam, Matinée Sunday 28th June 2026
